summary: Cathy O'Neil exposes how academic researchers across multiple disciplines accepted money from the Consumer Credit Research Foundation, a payday lending lobbying group, in exchange for allowing industry lawyers to edit their research papers. The investigation, conducted by Freakonomics through FOIA requests, reveals that one economist allowed CCRF lawyers to write entire paragraphs of pro-payday lending propaganda that appeared verbatim in his published work, despite claiming the organization had no editorial control. This compromised research was then used to lobby state legislatures, where most payday lending regulation occurs. O'Neil argues this undermines public trust in academic objectivity and creates additional obstacles for regulators like the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau.
key_points: Academic researchers from economics, law, data science, and management took money from payday lending lobbyists for favorable research
FOIA requests revealed that industry lawyers wrote entire paragraphs that appeared verbatim in supposedly independent academic papers
The compromised research was used to lobby state legislatures, which control most payday lending regulation
Only 40% of the implicated academics are economists, suggesting the corruption extends beyond the discipline already tainted by Inside Job
Some institutions are legally blocking FOIA requests, suggesting more compromised research may exist
money_quotes: We like to think of academic researchers as fair, objective, and politically neutral.
Even though we've all seen Inside Job and know not to trust economists, the rest of academia seems relatively safe. Right?
Email chains that show Fusaro let a lawyer from CCRF write whole paragraphs of pro-payday lending propaganda that made it verbatim into his paper, decrying the phrase 'cycle of debt' as meaningless.
This is in spite of Fusaro's claim in this same paper that CCRF had held no editorial control.
The statehouse is the end-goal for such lobbyists.
The CFPB has enough work on its hands without so-called impartial research being compromised left and right.
key_insight: Academic research credibility is being systematically corrupted by industry money, with lobbyists literally writing passages in supposedly independent papers to influence state-level regulation.
